worth_it_explanation: Third-party audience data from the Data Marketplace adds cost to every impression in an ad group where it is applied, so poorly chosen or redundant segments inflate the effective CPM without improving performance. These checks do not require pulling reports The Trade Desk restricts to partner logins. The segment browser, the ad group's data cost summary, and the platform's performance reporting give a practitioner enough to judge whether a segment is earning its CPM uplift, whether segments overlap with one another, and whether the total data cost is proportionate to what the targeting is achieving.
intro: The Trade Desk Data Marketplace lets advertisers layer third-party audience segments from data providers, including intent, behavioral, demographic, and contextual data, directly onto ad groups. Each segment added carries a cost-per-mille (CPM) uplift that compounds when multiple segments are stacked on the same ad group. These checks evaluate whether data selections are purposeful and proportionate, whether segment performance justifies data cost, and whether redundant or overlapping segments are inflating cost without improving reach or conversion rates.

audit_checks.title: Third-party audience segments are stacked broadly rather than selected with intent
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Each segment added to an ad group imposes a CPM uplift. Stacking several segments narrows the addressable audience while multiplying data costs on every remaining impression, raising the effective cost per conversion without a corresponding performance gain.

educate.base:
The Trade Desk Data Marketplace offers audience segments organized broadly by intent, behavioral, demographic, and contextual category, sourced from third-party data providers. When a practitioner selects segments for an ad group, the platform applies a CPM uplift for each segment that matches a bid request. Stacking several segments on the same ad group compounds those uplifts, so the effective cost per mille for matched impressions rises with each additional layer.

The IAB Tech Lab maintains an Audience Taxonomy that organizes segment types into demographic, interest, and purchase-intent tiers. Segments in the purchase-intent tier are designed to narrow to users actively signaling a decision, while interest and demographic segments are broader. Using a mix without a clear hierarchy means paying intent-tier prices for broad demographic reach, or vice versa.

A few signs point to unintentional stacking:
  • An ad group carries segments from different providers that describe the same audience from different angles, such as an in-market automotive segment from one provider alongside a car-enthusiast behavioral segment from another
  • The segment count on an ad group has grown over time as new segments were added without retiring old ones
  • The data cost line in the ad group's cost summary is a substantial share of total spend without a corresponding improvement in conversion rate or cost-per-acquisition (CPA) versus ad groups without data layering
You may want to review the segment list on each ad group against the campaign's audience strategy before adding further layers. A single well-matched intent segment often delivers more lift at lower data cost than several broad ones stacked together.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On an ecommerce campaign, purchase-intent segments sourced from retail data providers tend to match the purchase funnel more directly than broad interest or demographic segments. You may want to confirm that data selections map to a defined stage in the purchase path rather than adding layers across multiple intent categories simultaneously.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation campaign, professional or intent-based segments relevant to the decision-maker profile carry more precision than demographic layers added as reach extenders. You may want to confirm that each segment on the ad group corresponds to a specific audience hypothesis, and that the hypothesis has been tested before adding further segments.

investigate.intro: Review the segment selections on each ad group that uses Data Marketplace data, the data cost as a share of total spend, and whether performance metrics differ from ad groups without third-party data.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign, navigate to the ad group level, and open the targeting settings for an ad group that uses Data Marketplace segments.
Review the segment list. Note how many segments are applied, which providers they come from, and whether multiple segments appear to describe the same audience type from different angles.
Open the ad group's cost summary or budget pacing view and locate the data cost line. Note the data cost as a percentage of total ad group spend.

Compare performance metrics, such as conversion rate and CPA, for ad groups with multiple data segments versus ad groups with one segment or none. A wide gap in data cost with no corresponding performance improvement suggests the additional layers are not earning their cost.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per ad group using Data Marketplace segments, the segment count, the data cost as a share of total spend, and whether a clear audience hypothesis exists for each segment. An ad group with one or two purposefully chosen segments, a data cost share in line with the campaign's strategy, and measurable performance lift relative to non-data ad groups passes. An ad group with several segments from multiple providers describing similar audiences, a high data cost share, and no clear performance advantage over non-data ad groups is a fail recorded with the ad group name, the segment list, and the data cost percentage.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the segment list, data cost share, and performance comparison for each ad group above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that ad groups on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk campaigns carry multiple third-party audience segments stacked without a clear audience strategy, which compounds the CPM uplift on every matched impression. The data cost is a meaningful share of total spend on these ad groups with no corresponding improvement in conversion rate or cost-per-acquisition relative to ad groups using a single segment or none. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the segment list on each affected ad group, retaining the one or two segments best aligned to a defined audience hypothesis, and removing layers that duplicate audience intent from different providers. After consolidation, monitoring the data cost share and conversion rate for several weeks will confirm whether the reduction in data spend improves or maintains return on ad spend.

audit_checks.title: Data Marketplace segment cost is not justified by segment performance
audit_checks.impact_rationale: A segment that adds CPM uplift without improving conversion rate raises the effective cost per acquisition on every impression where it matches. The cost accrues continuously and is not visible as a separate line item in standard campaign reporting, so it can persist for months without a clear signal that it is underperforming.

educate.base:
The Trade Desk's Koa optimization engine allocates budget toward impressions most likely to convert, using signals including audience segment membership. When a third-party segment is applied, the platform pays the segment's CPM uplift on every impression where that segment matches a user. That cost is layered on top of the media cost before Koa's bidding logic applies.

A segment earns its cost when the conversion rate or return on ad spend (ROAS) on impressions matched to that segment is meaningfully higher than the conversion rate on unmatched impressions. A segment that does not improve the downstream metric relative to its cost is adding spend without adding performance. The Trade Desk surfaces segment-level performance data within the ad group reporting, which lets a practitioner compare matched and unmatched impressions directly.

A few signs suggest a segment is not earning its cost:
  • The conversion rate on impressions matched to a segment is at or below the conversion rate on impressions without that segment
  • Removing the segment from a test ad group produces no decline in conversion volume or ROAS over a comparable period
  • The data cost per conversion on the affected ad group is higher than the data cost per conversion on an ad group using no third-party data
You may want to evaluate each segment against a clear performance threshold before renewing it, rather than retaining it because it was added in a prior period.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On an ecommerce campaign, the clearest measure of segment value is the revenue or ROAS generated on impressions matched to that segment versus unmatched impressions. You may want to pull segment-level ROAS from the ad group's performance report and compare it against the blended campaign ROAS before deciding whether a segment's CPM uplift is justified.
site_type_overlays.lead_gen: On a lead-generation campaign, the relevant measure is the cost per qualified lead on segment-matched versus unmatched impressions. You may want to confirm that the lead quality attributed to segment-matched impressions is tracked back to the CRM before drawing a conclusion from top-of-funnel conversion counts alone.

investigate.intro: Pull segment-level performance data for each active Data Marketplace segment, compare matched versus unmatched conversion rates, and calculate whether the data cost per conversion is proportionate to the performance lift the segment provides.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the campaign and navigate to the ad group that carries Data Marketplace segments. Open the ad group's performance report and filter or break out results by audience segment where the platform allows.
For each segment, note the impressions matched, the conversion count or conversion rate, and the data cost attributed to that segment. Calculate the data cost per conversion for each segment.

Compare the data cost per conversion on segment-matched impressions to the overall cost per conversion on the ad group. A segment whose data cost per conversion is higher than the overall ad group cost per conversion is adding spend without improving the acquisition unit economics.

If segment-level breakdowns are not available directly in the reporting view, consider running a brief holdout test by duplicating the ad group without the segment applied and comparing performance over a period of at least two weeks with equal budget.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records, per segment, the data cost per conversion and whether that figure is lower than the ad group's overall cost per conversion. A segment whose matched impressions deliver a lower cost per conversion than the ad group average, or a materially higher ROAS, passes. A segment whose data cost per conversion exceeds the ad group average with no corresponding quality improvement is a fail, recorded with the segment name, the provider, the data cost percentage, and the performance comparison.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record the segment name, data cost per conversion, and comparison to the ad group average above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that one or more Data Marketplace segments applied to ad groups in {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk campaigns are not earning their CPM uplift. The data cost per conversion on impressions matched to these segments exceeds the ad group's overall cost per conversion, meaning the segments are raising acquisition costs rather than reducing them. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the performance data for each active segment against a defined cost-per-conversion threshold, removing segments that do not meet that threshold, and treating any future segment additions as a test with a defined evaluation window rather than a standing inclusion. Reducing data costs on underperforming segments frees budget for media that is already converting.

audit_checks.title: Overlapping or redundant Data Marketplace segments are not consolidated
audit_checks.impact_rationale: Redundant segments from different providers describing the same audience type pay duplicate CPM uplifts on the same users without expanding reach or improving match quality. The cost is real; the incremental audience is not.

educate.base:
The Trade Desk Data Marketplace carries segments from multiple third-party providers, and different providers often model the same audience type, such as in-market automotive intenders or frequent business travelers, using their own methodologies and data sources. When two or more segments describing the same audience are applied to an ad group, the platform pays a CPM uplift for each segment separately on impressions where the user appears in both.

The overlap does not expand reach. A user who qualifies for both a sports enthusiast segment from one provider and a sports content consumer segment from another is still one user. The second segment adds no incremental reach while adding incremental cost. The practical effect is a higher effective cost per mille on a fixed addressable audience.

A few signs point to segment redundancy:
  • Two or more segments on an ad group share similar names or descriptions, differing primarily in the provider name
  • Segment audience size estimates in the platform's segment browser are similar in magnitude, suggesting the underlying user pools overlap substantially
  • Removing one of two similar segments in a test ad group produces no measurable change in reach or conversion volume
You may want to consolidate redundant segments to the single provider whose historical performance on this account is strongest, rather than maintaining both to avoid a perceived risk of narrowing reach. The reach is already the same; the cost is not.

site_type_overlays.ecommerce: On an ecommerce campaign, redundant intent segments from different retail data providers are a common pattern, particularly for categories such as apparel, electronics, or home goods where multiple providers model in-market intent from different purchase-signal sources. You may want to identify which provider's segment has a stronger track record on purchase conversion before consolidating to one.

investigate.intro: Review the segment list on each ad group for pairs or groups of segments from different providers that describe the same audience type, and check whether removing one produces a measurable change in reach.

the_trade_desk_ui.label: The Trade Desk UI
steps: Open the ad group's targeting settings and review the full segment list. Group segments by audience type, such as in-market category, behavioral category, or demographic attribute, rather than by provider.
Identify any groups where two or more segments describe the same audience type from different providers. Note the estimated audience size for each segment in the segment browser.

For each redundant pair, compare performance metrics in the ad group's reporting. If one segment consistently produces lower cost per conversion than the other, that segment is the candidate to retain.

Consider running a test ad group with only the stronger-performing segment applied to confirm that reach and conversion volume are comparable before removing the redundant segment from the primary ad group.

evaluate.logic: The practitioner records whether any ad group carries two or more segments from different providers that describe the same audience type. An ad group with no redundant segments, or one where a redundancy test confirmed negligible incremental reach from the duplicate, passes. An ad group with confirmed redundant segments where both are still active and paying separate CPM uplifts is a fail, recorded with the ad group name, the redundant segment pair, and the estimated cost duplication.
evaluate.fallback_message: Record whether redundant segment pairs were identified and the outcome of any reach test above to see the evaluation.

generate.narrative_template: {{voice_found}} that ad groups on {{site_name}}'s The Trade Desk campaigns carry overlapping audience segments from different Data Marketplace providers describing the same audience type. The duplicate segments pay separate CPM uplifts on the same users without expanding the addressable audience, raising data costs on a fixed pool of impressions. {{voice_recommend}} reviewing the segment list on affected ad groups, identifying redundant pairs, retaining the segment from the provider with the stronger historical performance on this account, and removing the duplicate. Consolidating to one segment per audience type reduces data cost without reducing reach.
